multiplechoiceresponse.label: A city uses a risk model to send police patrols based on prior arrest data. Over time, Neighborhood X is policed more heavily, generating more arrests. What does this illustrate?

choicegroup.choice: Equal policing, since all neighborhoods are evaluated with the same model.
Fairness through blindness, since the model doesn’t use neighborhood labels directly.
A feedback loop that amplifies initial disparities.
Overfitting due to using too much historical arrest data.

div.p: This is a classic feedback loop, exactly as described in Lecture 2’s predictive policing example. Increased policing leads to more observed arrests, which then reinforces the model’s belief that the neighborhood is high risk. The updated data causes even more policing, further amplifying the original disparity. Using the same model or omitting neighborhood labels does not prevent this effect, because the data itself is shaped by past decisions.
